---
title: "Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy"
chapter_id: "verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "vcm_public"
  - "spinoza"
  - "treellm"
  - "vcm_editable"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Assigned source texts have not yet been ingested or summarized."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run."
  - "Chapter prose is a scaffold, not a completed manuscript draft."
---

# Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`, `vcm_editable` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source ingestion state | Source records assigned; source texts not yet ingested. |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run. |

## Drafting guardrail

This stub is derived from `book_structure.json` and the source inventory only. It does not claim that the listed source documents have been ingested, summarized, or independently verified.

## Problem

Long context does not automatically provide long-range reasoning or enough verification capacity.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Adding tokens can increase distractors, attention interference, and unverified summary burden.

## Core Claim

[verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate.

## Mechanism

- Model verification as bounded by mutual information, representation quality, and attention budget.
- Require adequacy labels for context packets.
- Route high-risk claims to stronger verification instead of more context alone.

## Interfaces

- VCM declares adequacy.
- Spinoza checks claims.
- Planning decides escalation when adequacy is insufficient.

## Invariants

- Adequacy is explicit.
- More context is not treated as proof.
- High-risk claims pay verification tax.

## Failure modes

- Attention interference.
- False confidence from large context.
- Verification skipped for cost reasons.

## Minimal implementation

A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Distractor resistance test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Adequacy labeling test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Verification escalation test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Planned use |
|---|---|---|
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. |
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. |
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. |

## Summary

This chapter currently establishes the structural slot for this layer of the ASI stack. The next drafting pass should ingest assigned sources, separate source-derived claims from design hypotheses, and update Appendix C when support states change.
